briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 21, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Climate Change Week: Nationwide Commitment to Carbon Neutrality Actions Spreads
sections.content: Today, in observance of Climate Change Week and Earth Day, various events encouraging climate action and carbon neutrality practices were held across the nation. Gwanak-gu and Siheung-si pledged energy conservation through lights-out events, while ICC JEJU, the Wonju Regional Environmental Office, Jangseong-gun, and others raised awareness for environmental protection through marine cleanup activities and various campaigns [2, 6, 7, 9, 11, 17, 18, 19, 23]. Notably, Geoje-si is actively pursuing the construction of a 47.2 billion won 'Climate Change Experiential Tourism Garden Dome' in preparation for the opening of the Southern Inland Railway, simultaneously pursuing two goals: climate change adaptation and tourism content development [1, 13, 15, 21, 22]. This widespread participation reflects the high level of interest from local communities and citizens in responding to the climate crisis and is interpreted as a positive signal for a sustainable future [4, 5, 8, 10].

sections.title: ⚠️ Domestic Carbon Neutrality Policies: Performance Review and Critical Voices from Civil Society
sections.content: Domestically, various policy efforts are underway to achieve carbon neutrality goals. Pyeongtaek-si has officially launched the '2050 Carbon Neutrality Green Growth Committee' to strengthen the implementation of carbon neutrality policies, and Chungnam Province is reviewing the progress of its carbon neutrality basic plan, indicating a strengthening of local government response systems [31, 35, 39, 46, 47]. Furthermore, concrete action plans are being explored, such as carbon neutrality education for farmer learning groups [29] and the promotion of mobile greenhouse gas observation projects [48]. However, the Daegu Climate Crisis Emergency Action group has given Daegu City's carbon neutrality policies a 'failing grade,' strongly advocating for a policy shift, and civil society organizations have stated their support for candidates who present climate pledges, indicating a growing chorus of critical voices from civil society [28, 34]. This, coupled with survey results indicating that Korean travelers perceive the impacts of climate change more significantly than the global average [20], and an op-ed [3] suggesting that an accurate understanding of the causes must precede finding solutions in the era of climate crisis, implies the need for in-depth discussions on the effectiveness of domestic policies.

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition: Active Corporate Investment and International Cooperation
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is accelerating both domestically and internationally, with direct investment and participation from corporations being particularly prominent. Naver acquired a 30% stake in a GS wind power plant and signed a direct power purchase agreement to stabilize the renewable energy supply for its data centers, marking the first direct investment case among Korean RE100 member companies [57, 59, 60, 63, 67, 70, 81, 87]. GS E&C is expanding its renewable energy business by building a hybrid model combining solar and wind power in India [61, 62, 71, 73, 76, 80, 82, 83, 84], and discussions on renewable energy cooperation between Korean and Ethiopian companies are also active [72]. The government is also expanding policy support, such as revising livestock support project guidelines to grant extra points for the adoption of renewable energy [65], and the UK is also pushing to reform the 'gas-linked structure' of electricity tariffs and expand fixed-price renewable energy contracts [64, 121]. These movements, along with the spread of renewable energy adoption in various sectors such as the construction of solar power infrastructure at Yeosu-Gwangyang Port [68], are becoming a significant driving force for the transition to a carbon-neutral era.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global Climate Change Impacts: Extended Allergy Seasons and Migration Issues
sections.content: Globally, the direct impacts of climate change are affecting daily life, particularly in the Duluth region of the United States and in Europe, where pollen allergy seasons are extending by up to five weeks, exacerbating citizens' suffering [88, 91, 109, 110, 111]. In Central America, reports indicate that migration is occurring due to pressures from climate change, making the issue of climate refugees a stark reality [93]. Furthermore, climate change is causing various social and environmental problems such as food supply issues [115], threats to student athlete safety [118], and wildfires that rage fiercely even at night [116], demanding adaptation efforts from humanity. These phenomena, coupled with a strong El Niño event and the forecast that 2026 will be the second warmest year on record [119, 122], further underscore the urgency of an international and integrated response to the climate crisis.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted global concerns about climate change, alongside diverse domestic and international response efforts. We must continue to build a better future through sustained attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.
